title: How LLMs Actually Think
description: Anthropic researchers Sholto Douglas and Trenton Bricken give a deep, wide-ranging context dump on how LLMs are trained, what capabilities they are likely to gain soon, and what is actually going on inside them.
longDescription: In this Dwarkesh Podcast conversation, Sholto Douglas and Trenton Bricken of Anthropic offer one of the clearest available walkthroughs of how large language models are trained, what they can and cannot do, and how researchers reason about what is happening inside them. The discussion ranges across model capabilities, training dynamics, and mechanistic interpretability, making it a rich primer for anyone who wants an insider's mental model of modern LLMs.

title: Is RL + LLMs Enough for AGI?
description: Sholto Douglas (scaling RL) and Trenton Bricken (mechanistic interpretability) of Anthropic discuss what changed in the last year of AI research, the new RL regime and how far it can scale, tracing a model's thoughts, and how to prepare for AGI.
longDescription: Anthropic's Sholto Douglas and Trenton Bricken return to the Dwarkesh Podcast to discuss what has changed over the past year in AI research. They cover the new reinforcement-learning regime and how far it can scale, how interpretability can trace a model's thoughts, and how countries, workers, and students should prepare for AGI. Sholto focuses on scaling RL and Trenton on mechanistic interpretability, giving a grounded look at where frontier research is heading.

title: We're Summoning Ghosts, Not Building Animals
description: Andrej Karpathy on why reinforcement learning is terrible (but everything else is worse), why AGI may blend into centuries of steady economic growth, why self-driving took so long, and the future of education.
longDescription: In this Dwarkesh Podcast episode, Andrej Karpathy shares his characteristically clear and contrarian views on the state of AI. He explains why he thinks reinforcement learning is deeply flawed yet still better than the alternatives, why AGI is likely to blend into the long historical trend of steady economic growth rather than arrive as a sudden break, why self-driving took so long to crack, and how he sees the future of education evolving. A wide-ranging, big-picture conversation from one of the field's clearest thinkers.

title: From the Age of Scaling to the Age of Research
description: Ilya Sutskever on why the field is moving from the age of scaling to the age of research: SSI's strategy, the limits of pre-training, improving generalization, and how to make AGI go well.
longDescription: In this Dwarkesh Podcast conversation, Ilya Sutskever argues that AI is shifting from an era defined by scaling to one defined by research. He discusses the strategy behind Safe Superintelligence (SSI), the limitations of pre-training, how to improve the generalization of AI models, and what it will take to ensure that AGI goes well. A thoughtful, forward-looking discussion from one of the most influential researchers in deep learning.

title: LLM Reasoning
description: Denny Zhou (Google DeepMind) on what makes LLMs reason: why generating intermediate steps helps, how chain-of-thought can be elicited from pretrained models, and the key ideas behind improving reasoning in large language models.
longDescription: In this Stanford CS25 lecture, Denny Zhou, who leads reasoning research at Google DeepMind, distills key insights on reasoning in large language models. He discusses why generating intermediate steps (chain-of-thought) dramatically improves performance, how reasoning can be elicited from pretrained models through prompting and decoding, the role of self-consistency and answer aggregation, and what current results suggest about the path toward more capable reasoning systems. The talk offers a clear, research-grounded mental model of how and why LLMs reason.

title: The Bitter Lesson
description: Rich Sutton's influential 2019 essay arguing that the biggest lesson from 70 years of AI research is that general methods that leverage computation, namely search and learning, ultimately win out over approaches that build in human knowledge.
longDescription: In this short essay, Richard Sutton argues that the most important lesson from decades of AI research is that general-purpose methods that scale with computation (namely search and learning) consistently outperform systems that rely on hand-crafted human knowledge. Time and again, researchers have built in domain knowledge for short-term gains, only to be overtaken by simpler approaches that better exploit ever-growing compute. The piece has become a touchstone in debates about scaling, foundation models, and the future direction of AI research.

title: The Parable of the Parser
description: Ross Girshick talk critiques legacy paradigms in NLP and computer vision, advocating for a shift toward real-world, open-ended AI tasks like VQA and embodied vision.
longDescription: The Parable of the Parser by Ross Girshick, is a reflective talk from CVPR 2024 that discusses the evolution of NLP and computer vision. It examines the shift from classical parsing-based approaches in NLP to the dominance of LLMs, emphasizing that text generation has become the primary goal. The talk draws parallels to computer vision, questioning what the equivalent of parsers are in CV and advocating for a shift toward real-world, open-ended visual tasks such as visual question answering (VQA) and embodied vision.

title: State of GPT
description: Andrej Karpathy discusses the training pipeline of GPT assistants, from tokenization to pretraining, supervised finetuning, and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF).
longDescription: In this talk, Andrej Karpathy provides insights into the rapidly evolving ecosystem of large language models. He explains the four major stages of training GPT assistants: pretraining, supervised finetuning, reward modeling, and reinforcement learning. The presentation also covers practical techniques for using these models effectively, including prompting strategies and the future extensions of the GPT ecosystem.

title: Agents for Software Development
description: Graham Neubig discusses the role of AI agents in software development, focusing on their potential to automate tasks like code generation and bug fixing.
longDescription: In this talk, Graham Neubig explores the capabilities and challenges of using AI agents for software development. He highlights how agents can assist in tasks such as coding, bug fixing, and testing, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to generate code and interact with software environments. Neubig also addresses key challenges like file localization, planning, error recovery, and safety, emphasizing the importance of open-source tools like OpenHands for advancing agent capabilities.
event: Berkeley RDI Center on Decentralization & AI

title: Deep Residual Learning and How It Shaped the AI Landscape
description: Dr. Kaiming He discusses the impact of deep residual learning on the AI landscape, highlighting its role in enabling deeper neural networks.
longDescription: In this lecture, Dr. He shares insights into how deep residual learning has revolutionized artificial intelligence by allowing neural networks to achieve unprecedented depths. He explains how this innovation has led to significant breakthroughs in various AI applications, including AlphaGo, AlphaFold, and ChatGPT. The talk emphasizes the foundational role of deep residual learning in modern AI advancements. He starts at 19:22.

title: You and Your Research
description: Richard Hamming discusses the traits and habits necessary for conducting great research, focusing on drive, emotional commitment, and strategic effort.
longDescription: In this talk, Hamming explores why some scientists achieve significant contributions while others do not. He emphasizes the importance of drive, emotional commitment to one's work, and strategic effort. Hamming notes that luck plays a role but is not the primary factor; rather, being prepared and seizing opportunities is crucial. He also highlights the need to ask important questions, work on significant problems, and maintain a balance between confidence and doubt to foster creativity and progress.

title: Learning to Reason with LLMs
description: This video is a presentation titled "Learning to Reason with LLMs," given at a Simons Institute event, featuring Noam Brown from OpenAI.
longDescription: Naom Brown discusses his background in AI, particularly his work on poker-playing AI, and then explores the development and implications of the o1 model from OpenAI. Key points include the impact of search/planning in AI game playing (poker, Go), the introduction of the 01 model which scales inference compute using reinforcement learning to refine its "chain of thought", allowing for error correction and better problem-solving. Brown suggests this approach opens new avenues for AI progress beyond pre-training costs and mentions a new focus on multi-agent reasoning at OpenAI.

title: Keynote IV: Teaching LLMs to Reason
description: What is required to make LLMs useful scientific assistants? In this talk we cover how LLMs perform on scientific reasoning tasks, including some recent results.
longDescription: What is required to make LLMs useful scientific assistants? In this talk we cover how LLMs perform on scientific reasoning tasks, including some recent results.
event: 2nd ICML Workshop on Machine Learning for Astrophysics (ICML 2023)
